what_supported_the_under: Minnesota needed 48 second-half points to defeat the team-total wager after scoring 47 in the first half
The Lynx had eight first-half turnovers, creating an apparent path toward empty possessions
Portland had recently shown improved defensive form and held Minnesota to 23 points in the second quarter
The market line provided a small buffer above a simple first-half doubling projection of 94
why_the_under_failed: Minnesota's first-half process was already highly efficient: 55.6 percent shooting, 30 paint points on 15-for-21 shooting and 14 assists
The game was not truly slow. Estimated first-half pace was approximately 82.6 possessions over a full game, so the low free-throw volume disguised an ordinary-to-fast possession environment
Only six total first-half free throws created substantial whistle and free-throw regression risk for the second half
Minnesota committed zero turnovers in the third quarter and scored 28 points, immediately removing most of the Under's margin
Portland remained competitive enough to retain Minnesota's primary rotation and offensive urgency rather than creating a low-intensity closeout
The fourth quarter became a high-efficiency shooting exchange: Portland scored 33 on 68.4 percent shooting and Minnesota scored 26 on 61.1 percent shooting
Minnesota finished with 29 assists, 54 paint points and a 122.1 estimated offensive rating, confirming that the offense was structurally clean rather than merely running hot

decision_grade.classification: Under positions entered without sufficient edge against a healthy-efficiency and normal-pace environment
decision_grade.variance_component: Moderate - Portland's 70 percent fourth-quarter three-point shooting was extreme, but Minnesota's team-total loss was already threatened by strong paint efficiency and ball movement
decision_grade.primary_process_error: The model treated low first-half scoring and low free-throw volume as under evidence while underweighting possession count, paint conversion, assist quality, zero-turnover upside and competitive-game starter retention

counterfactual.recommended_minimum_team_total_edge: At least 4.0 projected points after pace, shot-quality and rotation adjustments

model_lessons: Separate scoring pace from possession pace; low free-throw volume can suppress the scoreboard without indicating a slow game
At halftime, calculate estimated possessions and offensive efficiency before using raw score as the primary anchor
Flag teams shooting at least 55 percent with 70 percent-plus paint conversion and strong assist creation as unsafe team-total Under candidates
Treat unusually low first-half free-throw volume as second-half Over risk unless the officiating crew and rim-attempt profile support persistence
Do not count turnovers as future Under support without testing whether they are stable, lineup-driven or likely to regress
Apply a competitive-game retention adjustment when the favorite leads by fewer than 12 points at halftime
Require at least a four-point model cushion for live team totals; a projected number near the sportsbook line is a pass, not an investment
Do not stack a pregame game Under and a correlated live favorite team-total Under unless the live evidence independently strengthens the original thesis

summary: Minnesota defeated Portland 101-93, producing 194 total points. The pregame Under 173.5 lost by 20.5 points, and the halftime Minnesota team-total Under 94.5 lost by 6.5 points after the Lynx scored 54 in the second half. The central leak was not simply bad fourth-quarter variance. At halftime, Minnesota was already shooting 55.6 percent, converting 15 of 21 paint attempts and generating 14 assists at an estimated full-game pace above 82 possessions. The model over-weighted the raw 86-point halftime score and eight Minnesota turnovers while under-weighting shot quality, low free-throw regression risk, turnover regression and starter retention. This should have been a pass at 94.5, and the correlated exposure to both a pregame game Under and a live Minnesota team-total Under amplified the loss.
